gui_add_ui [-help] name [-before name ] [-checked {true | false}] [-command string ] [-disabled {true | false}] [-enable_variable string ] [-foreground string ] [-icon filename ] [-in name ] [-label string ] [-newline {true | false}] [-shortcut string ] [-tooltip string ] -type {menu | toolbar | submenu | command | check | radio | separator | toolbutton} [-underline integer ] [-value string ] [-variable string ] [-visible_variable string ]

Adds a new interface element with the specified name of the specified type. Use this command to customize the graphical interface by adding menus, toolbars, status bar, and other interface elements, as required.

data:
Prints a brief description that includes type and default information for each gui_add_ui parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command: 
 man gui_add_ui

Specifies as a string the internal name of the interface element you want to add. 
 Note: Choose the internal name of the element carefully as this is what is used internally by the software to identify the element for any further manipulation.
When specified, adds the new element before the element identified by name .

Specifies whether or not the check box or radio button you are adding should be checked. 
 Note: You can use the -checked parameter only if you are adding elements of type check or radio . 
 Default : false

Specifies the Tcl command to be executed when the element you are adding is invoked. 
 Note: You can use the -command parameter only if you are adding elements of type command, check, radio or toolbutton .

Specifies the state of the interface element you are adding. 
 Note: You can use the -disabled parameter only if you are adding elements of type menu, submenu, command, check, radio or toolbutton . The parameter is NOT applicable for elements of type toolbar and separator . 
 Default : false
Represents the Tcl global variable for Enable status.

Specifies foreground color of the icon. 
 Note: You can use the -foreground parameter only if you are adding an element of type toolbutton .

When adding a toolbutton, specifies the file name of the toolbutton icon. filename can be the full path of the icon file or an icon name supported by the software. 
 The icon names used in the software can be queried using the gui_get_ui command. 
 Note: The -icon parameter is applicable only for elements of type toolbutton .

Specifies the name of the parent element. Use this parameter along with -before to specify the location of the element you are adding. By default, -in adds the element to the end of the parent element. Use -before to add the element at a specific location within the parent element. 
 Note: If -in is not specified, the new element is created but is not added to any location.

Specifies the label to be displayed on the new element. 
 Note: This parameter is NOT applicable for elements of type separator .

When adding a toolbar, specifies whether or not the toolbar should be added as a new row. 
 Note: The -newline parameter is applicable only for elements of type toolbar . 
 Default : false

Specifies the shortcut key for a command, check box, radio button, or toolbutton. 
 Note: You can use the -shortcut parameter only if you are adding elements of type command , check , radio , or toolbutton .

Specifies the tooltip text for the toolbar or toolbutton you are adding. 
 Note: The -tooltip parameter is applicable only for elements of type toolbar and toolbutton .
-type {menu | toolbar | submenu | command | check | radio | separator | toolbutton}
Specifies the type of the interface element you want to add. Currently, elements of type main and statusbar are not supported.

Specifies the integer index of the character to be underlined in the element label. The index begins with 0 and should have a value less than or equal to the length of the element label. For instance, if you want the second letter of the element label to appear underlined, specify integer as 1 . 
 Note: The -underline parameter is NOT applicable for elements of type toolbar, separator, and toolbutton . 
 Note: This parameter can be used only if the -label parameter is also specified.

When adding a radio button, specifies the value to be stored in the associated variable when the radio button is selected. 
 Note: The -value parameter is applicable only for elements of type radio .
When adding a check box or radio button, specifies the Tcl global variable for the item.
Represents the Tcl global variable for Visible status

- The following command will add a new menu item labeled ExampleCommand in ExampleMenu : gui_add_ui exmpCommand -type command -label ExampleCommand -in exmpMenu -command [list puts Example Command]
Attaches selected or specified objects to the cursor. The use model is as follows:

1. Select the object(s) to be moved by using the gui_attach_to_cursor command.
2. Zoom into the location where you want to place the object(s).
3. Click at the required location to place the selected object(s) there.
This command is useful during floorplanning when you may need to move multiple objects into the same zoomed in area.

 For a detailed description of the command, use the man command: man gui_attach_to_cursor .

Removes highlights from all the highlighted objects at any stage of the design flow. You can choose to clear highlight from all objects, selected objects, objects highlighted with original color, or objects with specified index values.

data:
Prints a brief description that includes type and default information for each gui_clear_highlight parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command: man gui_clear_highlight
Specifies that highlight will be cleared from all objects.

Specifies that highlight will be cleared from the objects with the specified index value. 
 The index value is the value of the Highlight Set as specified through the View - Highlight Selected menu or through the gui_highlight command. 
 The View - Highlight Selected menu provides eight highlight patterns to choose from. The value of index should, therefore, be a number be from 1 to 8. 
 As an example, if you specify a value of 3 for index, all highlight will be cleared from from all objects that are highlighted as Highlighted Set 3.
Specifies the object pointer list for clearing highlight.
Specifies that highlight should be cleared from the objects highlighted with original color.
Specifies that highlight should be cleared from all selected objects.

lines: - The following command clears highlight from all highlighted objects. gui_clear_highlight

- The following command clears highlight from all objects that have been highlighted as Highlight Set 4 through the View - Highlight Selected menu or through the gui_highlight command. gui_clear_highlight -index 4
- The following command clears highlight from all highlighted objects that are currently selected in the display window. gui_clear_highlight-select
gui_create_user_bundle_net [-help] name -nets net_list - start object_name -end object_name

Creates a bundle of specified nets that can be displayed as flightlines. Bundled nets help you with data flow analysis before and after layout. Net bundles can be created and viewed in floorplan and amoeba views. Each custom net bundle displays the number of nets in it and works for macros, blobs, and giudes.

Using gui_create_user_bundle_net , you can create custom bundled nets that allow you to define your own net bundles as per your requirements. This is useful while debugging physical properties of interconnects within a complicated hierarchical design because you can define start and end points of a net bundle. As a result, you can create and display only relevant flightlines, instead of looking at all the flightlines of specified nets.
Note: One net can be part of multiple net bundles simultaneously.
You can display flightline net bundles using the gui_show_user_bundle_nets command and delete them using the gui_delete_user_bundle_net command.

Note: You can choose to display flightlines for only bundled nets by using the Only Show Net Bundle Flight Line option in the Preferences form. If this option is not selected, the net bundle flightlines are displayed along with other selection-based flightlines by default.

data:
Prints a brief description that includes type and default information for each gui_create_user_bundle_net parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command: 
 man gui_create_user_bundle_net
Specifies the name of the custom net bundle.
Specifies the list of nets to be included in the net bundle. You can use patterns with wildcards to specify net names. All the nets that match the specified pattern are put in the same bundle.
Specifies the name of object which is the start point of the nets. The object type includes inst, hinst, port, base_pin, and group.
Specifies the name of object which is the end point of the nets. The object type includes inst, hinst, port, base_pin, and group.

lines: - The following command creates a bundle called bundle_1 , which contains flightlines of nets net_1 and net_2 : gui_create_user_bundle_net bundle_1 -nets {net_1 net_2} -start Module1 -end Module2 You can then use the gui_show_user_bundle_nets command to display bundle_1 flightlines as follows: gui_show_user_bundle_nets -net_bundle bundle_1

lines: Deletes an existing interface element with the specified name. If multiple interface elements are found with the same name, the first matched element is deleted. Use this command to customize the graphical interface by removing menus, menu items, and other interface elements that you do not require any longer.

Specifies the internal name of the interface element as a string. An error is returned if an interface element with the specified name does not exist. 
 Note: Here, name represents the internal name assigned to the element when it was added to the interface and not the label that is displayed on the interface. If you specify the label instead of the name, the software will return an error.

lines: - The following command will delete an interface element with the internal name Menu1 : gui_delete_ui Menu1
Deletes the specified flightline net bundle. If a net is part of multiple net bundles and you delete one of the net bundles, other net bundles containing the net are not affected.
You can create flightline net bundles using the gui_create_user_bundle_net command and display them using the gui_show_user_bundle_nets command.

Note: gui_delete_user_bundle_net removes the specified net bundle. If you want to retain the net bundle but just want to clear its flightlines from the display area, use the -remove option of gui_show_user_bundle_nets instead.

lines: gui_write_picture [-help] filename [-format {bmp gif jpg jpeg png pbm pgm ppm xbm xpm}] [-width integer_value -height integer_value ] [-window string ]

Saves a snapshot of the layout or specified window with the specified name in the current directory. You can specify the size as well as the format in which you want to save the snapshot. The command also prints out overlay maps with legends.
You can use this command at any stage of the design flow.

data:
Prints a brief description that includes type and default information for each gui_write_picture parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command: 
 man gui_write_picture

Specifies the name of the picture file as a string. You can specify the file extension as well. 
 Note : The file extension you specify does not control the format of the picture. The picture file is dumped in the PNG format by default or the format you explicitly specify with -format . For example, the following command saves the testShot picture file in the PNG format: > gui_write_picture testShot.gif 
 current_directory @file testShot.gif testShot.gif: PNG image data, 540 x 688, 8-bit/color RGB, non-interlaced
-format {bmp gif jpg jpeg png pbm pgm ppm xbm xpm}

Specifies the format of the picture file. 
 Default : png

Specifies the height of the picture as an integral value. 
 Note : The - width parameter is required when - height is specified. 
 Min : 100 
 Max : 20000

Specifies the width of the picture as an integral value. 
 Note : The -height parameter is required when -width is specified. 
 Min : 100 
 Max : 20000

Specifies the ID of the window that you want to dump. You can use the following IDs: 
 For Layout Viewer: designview.win For Cell Viewer: .uiCellView.win 
 If -window is not specified, the main layout window is dumped out by default.

lines: - The following command captures the layout window and saves it in the default PNG format with the name test2 and the dimensions 1000 x 1000 in the current directory: gui_write_picture test2 -width 1000 height 1000

- The following command captures the layout window and saves it in the GIF format with the name test1.gif in the current directory: gui_write_picture -format gif test1.gif
- The following command captures the layout viewer window and saves it in PNG format with the name testShot.png in the current directory: gui_write_picture testShot.png -window designview.win
- The following command captures the view in the cell viewer window and saves it in PNG format with the name testCell.png in the current directory: gui_write_picture testCell.png -window .uiCellView.win
Enables you to retrieve an element's name by specifying a property-value combination.

data:
Prints a brief description that includes type and default information for each gui_find_ui parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command: 
 man gui_find_ui

Specifies the name of the parent element to define the search scope. If multiple elements are found with the same name , the first matched element is searched. If name is not specified, a global search is performed.

Specifies the property-value combination that should be used as the search criteria for finding an element. In addition to all the properties listed in the gui_add_ui command, -type type is also supported. 
 To see the list of properties supported by the gui_add_ui command, see gui_add_ui .

lines: - The following command returns the names of all the menus in the main window: gui_find_ui main -type menu The software returns the following: file view edit partition fplan power place eco clock route timing verify pvs tools windows flow help
- The following command returns the name of the element before the Help menu in the main window: gui_find_ui -before help The software returns the following: flow

lines: Retrieves the current value of the specified property of the element that is being queried. Using the gui_get_ui command, you can query all the properties in the gui_add_ui command for each type. In addition, depending on the type , some special properties are supported too as described below:
statusbar also supports the -enable_variable and -visible_variable parameters.

data:
Prints a brief description that includes type and default information for each gui_get_ui parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command: 
 man gui_get_ui

Specifies as a string the name of the interface element you want to query. If multiple widgets are found with the same name , the first matched widget is queried. Returns an error if there is no element with the specified name.

Specifies the property whose value is to be retrieved. You can specify the following properties: 
 -before | -checked | -children | -command | -disabled | -dockwin | -dock_widget | -enable_variable | -foreground | -geometry | -icon | -in | -label | -menu | -message | -newline | -shortcut | -statusbar | -title | -toolbar | -tooltip | -type | -underline | -value | -variable | -visible | -visible_variable
When specified, returns the element that occurs before the element identified by name .
Returns the on/off state of the specified check box or radio button. If the check box or radio button is selected, returns true . If it is deselected, returns false .

Returns child elements of specified interface element. 
 Note: You can use this parameter with elements of type menu , submenu , and toolbar .

Returns the Tcl command to be executed when the specified element you are adding is invoked. 
 Note: You can use the -command parameter only with elements of type command, check, radio or toolbutton .

When specified, returns whether the specified interface element is enabled ( false ) or disabled ( true ). 
 Note: You can use the -disabled parameter only with elements of type menu, submenu, command, check, radio or toolbutton . The parameter is NOT applicable for elements of type toolbar and separator .
Returns the dock widget in the window.
Represents the Tcl global variable for Enable status.

Returns the foreground color of the icon for the specified toolbutton. 
 Note: You can use the -foreground parameter only with elements of type toolbutton .

Returns the width, height, and coordinates of the main window. 
 Note: The -geometry parameter is applicable only for the main window, which is type main .

Returns the file name of the specified toolbutton. 
 Note: The -icon parameter is applicable only for elements of type toolbutton .
Returns the name of the parent element.

Returns the label of the specified element. 
 Note: This parameter is NOT applicable for elements of type separator .

Returns the names of the menus in the main window. 
 Note: This parameter is applicable only for the main window, which is type main .

Returns the message on the status bar. 
 Note: This parameter is applicable only for the status bar.

Returns whether or not the specified toolbar starts in a new row. 
 Note: The -newline parameter is applicable only for elements of type toolbar .

Returns the shortcut key for a command, check box, radio button, or toolbutton. 
 Note: You can use the -shortuct parameter only with elements of type command , check , radio , or toolbutton .

Returns the name of the main window's status bar. 
 Note: This parameter is applicable only for the main window ( main ).

Returns the text on the main window's title bar. 
 Note: This parameter is applicable only for the main window ( main ).
Returns the toolbars in the window.

Returns the tooltip text for the specified toolbar or toolbutton. 
 Note: The -tooltip parameter is applicable only for elements of type toolbar and toolbutton .
Returns the type of the specified element.

Returns the integer index of the underlined character in the element label. 
 Note: The -underline parameter is NOT applicable for elements of type toolbar, separator, and toolbutton .

Returns the value stored in the associated variable of the specified radio button. 
 Note: The -value parameter is applicable only for elements of type radio .
Returns the Tcl global variable for the specified check box or radio button.
Returns the visibility status of the element.
Represents the Tcl global variable for Visible status.
Displays the current settings for the specified parameters in the Tcl list format.

You can run the gui_show command to re-display the main window.

data: Prints out the command usage. For a detailed description of the command, use the man command: man gui_hide

lines: Highlights selected or specified objects with the Highlight Set available through the View - Highlight Selected menu. The value for the index corresponds to the Highlight Set that will be applied. For example, a value of 4 for the index specifies that all selected objects will be highlighted with the same color and pattern as available for the Highlight Set #4 in the View - Highlight Selected menu. You can use this command at any stage in the design flow.
You can also provide a specific color and/or pattern to be used. All selected objects will be highlighted with the color and/or pattern that you specify.

If you specify a color and or pattern, the Highlight Set corresponding to the index value will be updated to use the color and pattern specified. For example, if you specify an index value of 5, color green , and pattern backslash , the Highlight Set #5 will get updated to have the color green and the pattern backslash .
You can remove highlight from the highlighted objects through the gui_clear_highlight command or through the View - Clear Highlight menu.
Note: If an object is selectable, you can highlight it using the gui_highlight command.
Use the gui_highlight_pin command to highlight shapes for specified SIGNAL/POWER pins.

data:
Prints a brief description that includes type and default information for each gui_highlight parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command: man gui_highlight
Specifies the object pointer list for highlighting.

Specifies the object color automatically. 
 Note : This parameter cannot be used with -index or -color .

Specifies the index value for the specified object. 
 All objects that are selected will be highlighted with the same color and pattern as defined in corresponding Highlight Set in the View - Highlight Selected menu 
 As an example, if you specify a value of 3 for index, all objects that are selected will be highlighted with the same color and pattern as available for the Highlight Set #3 in the View - Highlight Selected menu. 
 The value of index should be between 1 to 64.

Specifies the color value for the specified object. The color value is the same as that available through the Edit Highlight Color form ( View - Edit Highlight Color ). 
 You can also use the Red Green Blue (RGB) value in the following format: # V1V2V3 where V1, V2, and V3 are the hexadecimal values for the red, green, and blue color components respectively. For example, you can specify the value #CC66FF . 
 Default: The color defined for the Highlight Set corresponding to the index value is used.

Highlights the object in original color. This option is useful if you want to check a specific object more closely with the background dimmed. An object highlighted in original color will stand out in the dimmed background: 
 
 Note : This parameter cannot be used with -index , -color , or -pattern .

Specifies the pattern value for the specified object. The pattern value is the same as the Stipple Value available through the Edit Highlight Color form. 
 Default: The pattern (Stipple Value) for the Highlight Set corresponding to the index value is used.

lines: - The following command highlights all selected objects with the same color and pattern as defined for the Highlight Set #4 in the View - Highlighted Selected menu. gui_highlight -index 4

- The following command highlights all selected objects with the color green and the pattern dot4 . The index values is set to 5 and, therefore, the command will also change the definition of the Highlight Set #5 ( View - Highlight Selected menu) to have color green and pattern dot4 . gui_highlight -index 5 -color green -pattern dot4

- The following command highlights all selected objects with the color corresponding to the RGB hexadecimal value #33FF66 and the pattern horizontal . The index values is set to 3 and, therefore, the command will also change the definition of the Highlight Set #5 ( View - Highlight Selected menu) to have color #33FF66 and pattern horizontal . gui_highlight -index 3 -color #33FF66 -pattern horizontal
gui_highlight_pin [-help] -inst string -pin string [-original | -index indexValue ] [-original | -color colorValue ] [-original | -pattern patternValue ]

Highlights the shapes of all specified SIGNAL/POWER instance pins with the Highlight Set available through the View - Highlight Selected menu. The value for the index corresponds to the Highlight Set that will be applied. For example, a value of 4 for the index specifies that all selected objects will be highlighted with the same color and pattern as available for the Highlight Set #4 in the View - Highlight Selected menu.
You can also provide a specific color and/or pattern to be used. All specified pins will be highlighted with the color and/or pattern that you specify.

Note: If you specify a color and or pattern, the Highlight Set corresponding to the index value will be updated to use the color and pattern specified. For example, if you specify an index value of 5 , color green , and pattern backslash , the Highlight Set #5 will get updated to have the color green and the pattern backslash .

data:
Prints a brief description that includes the type and default information for each gui_highlight_pin parameter. 
 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command: 
 man gui_h ighlight_pin

Specifies the color value for the specified object. The color value is the same as that available through the Edit Highlight Color form, which can be accessed by selecting View - Edit Highlight Color . 
 You can also use the Red Green Blue (RGB) value in the following format: # V1V2V3 where V1 , V2 , and V3 are the hexadecimal values for the Red, Green, and Blue color components, respectively. For example, you can specify the value #CC66FF . 
 Default: The color defined for the Highlight Set corresponding to the specified index value is used.

Specifies the index value for the specified object. 
 By default, all specified pins are highlighted with the same color and pattern as defined in the Highlight Set corresponding to the specified index value in the View - Highlight Selected submenu. 
 For example, if you specify a value of 3 for index, all specified pins are highlighted with the same color and pattern as available for Highlight Set #3 in the View - Highlight Selected submenu. 
 Note : The View - Highlight Selected submenu provides eight highlight patterns. Therefore, the value of the index should be a number from 1 to 8.

Highlights the instance pin in original color. This option is useful if you want to check a specific instance pin more closely with the background dimmed. An object highlighted in original color will stand out in the dimmed background. 
 Note : This parameter cannot be used with -index , -color , or -pattern .

Specifies the pattern value for the specified object. The pattern value is the same as the Stipple value available through the Edit Highlight Color form. 
 Default: The pattern ( Stipple value) defined for the Highlight Set corresponding to the specified index value is used.
Specifies the name of SIGNAL/POWER pin to be highlighted. You can specify more than one pin name.

lines:

- The following command highlights the SIGNAL pin E and the POWER pin gnd of instance U1 using the color yellow and the solid pattern: gui_h ighlight_pin -inst U1 -pin {E gnd} -color yellow -pattern solid -index 1

 man gui_open_schematic

Specifies that the new center of the viewing window should shift from the current center of the viewing window by ( x , y ) microns. Use this command at any stage in the design flow to pan or move the design view in a specific direction.

 For a detailed description of the command and all of its parameters, use the man command: man gui_pan .

Specifies that the design center should shift d x microns on the x-axis from the center of the viewing window. 
 You can specify positive or negative values for d x . A positive value specifies shifting to the right on the x axis.

Specifies that the design center should shift dy microns on the y axis values from the center of the viewing window. 
 You can specify positive or negative values for dy . A positive value specifies shifting up on the y axis.
